<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Fact-check every claim in docs">
You are a fact-checker. Your ONLY job is to verify that every factual claim in this project's documentation is true. Earlier in this project a plausible-sounding claim about a cited source was written into the docs and turned out to be FALSE (it said worldfootball.net sorts its table by goal difference; it doesn't). That claim was removed. Your job is to make sure no other false claim survives.

Project: /Users/jramirezolmos/Documents/personal/span-league-table

Check EVERY checkable claim in README.md, AI_REFLECTION.md, CLAUDE.md, the git commit messages (`git log`), and the docstrings/comments in src/ and tests/. For each claim, classify: TRUE (verified, say how), FALSE (with evidence), or UNVERIFIABLE.

Categories to check:

A) **Historical claims.** Verify against real sources:
   - "2 points for a win, 1 for a draw" in the English First Division 1974/75; three points introduced 1981/82.
   - Ties broken by goal AVERAGE (goals scored / goals conceded), and goal difference did not replace it until 1976/77.
   - The quoted sentences attributed to Wikipedia's "1974–75 Football League" and "1981–82 Football League" pages — are they VERBATIM and correctly attributed? Open the pages.
   - Claims about the 1974/75 fixture calendar: season started 17 Aug 1974; midweek rounds in the opening weeks; ten matchdays complete by 28 Sep 1974; the 10th calendar week ending 19 Oct 1974 (after round 14); six postponed fixtures (three from round 9, three from round 13) played as late as April 1975.

B) **Claims about cited sources.** The README cites worldfootball.net and 11v11.com. Open BOTH pages yourself in the browser and verify every statement made about them, including:
   - That 11v11's table for 19 Oct 1974 has a goal-average column, is ordered by it, and that our data/standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv reproduces it exactly (all 22 rows, same order).
   - That 11v11's table for 28 Sep 1974 corresponds to data/standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv.
   - The README's careful hedge that it does NOT claim to know what rule 11v11 uses for clubs tied on points AND goal average — check that this hedge is accurate (i.e. that alphabetical and goals-scored really would both produce the observed order for Burnley/Newcastle and Birmingham/Wolverhampton).
   - Anything said about worldfootball's row ordering.

C) **Claims about the code/tests.** Verify by running things:
   - The stated test count and "100% branch coverage" claim, and that the ONLY coverage exclusion is `if __name__ == "__main__":`.
   - README claims about behaviour: exit codes 0/2/141, in-place rewrite safety (-o same as input), BOM handling, rejection of duplicate header columns / wrong-width rows / non-ASCII-digit scores, the duplicate-fixture warning.
   - The claim that goal average and goal difference order Middlesbrough/Liverpool differently in the matchday-10 table, and that Tottenham/Arsenal/QPR all have -5 goal difference.
   - The claim in AI_REFLECTION that a deleted method broke the tie-break — is that consistent with the code as it stands (table.py)?

URLs (they 403 plain fetches — use the Chrome browser tools, load via ToolSearch: mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp, tabs_create_mcp, navigate, get_page_text):
- https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/
- https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-september-1974/
- https://www.worldfootball.net/competition/ro15215/md10/results-and-standings/
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Football_League
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Football_League

Do NOT modify anything in the project directory (read-only). Scratch work goes in /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-jramirezolmos-Documents-personal-span-league-table/ed232752-4a2f-47aa-9632-c8175fcab651/scratchpad.

Report: a list of every FALSE or MISLEADING claim (most serious first) with the exact file:line, the claim quoted, the evidence it is wrong, and the correction. Then a list of claims you verified as TRUE (brief). Then anything UNVERIFIABLE. If you cannot open a source, say so — do NOT guess, and do NOT assume a claim is true because it sounds right.
</teammate-message>

<system-reminder>
Other agents active in this session, addressable via SendMessage({to: name, message}): main, final-red-team, qa-reviewer, span-red-team.
</system-reminder>

content.content:
1	# League Standings — English First Division, 1974/75
2	
3	A command-line tool that reads football results from CSV and prints a league standing
4	table, using the rules in force in the English First Division in the 1974/75 season.
5	
6	The tool is general: it tabulates whatever results it is given. It knows nothing about
7	weeks, matchdays or 1974 — those live in the data, not the code.
8	
9	```
10	$ league-table --pretty data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv
11	Pos  Team                      P  W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
12	  1  Ipswich Town             10  8  0  2  18   6  3.000   16
13	  2  Newcastle United         10  6  2  2  19  14  1.357   14
14	  3  Manchester City          10  6  2  2  14  11  1.273   14
15	  4  Middlesbrough            10  5  3  2  15   7  2.143   13
16	  5  Liverpool                10  6  1  3  17   8  2.125   13
17	  ...
18	```
19	
20	## Setup
21	
22	Python 3.9+. No runtime dependencies; the tool itself is pure standard library.
23	
24	```bash
25	python3 -m venv .venv
26	source .venv/bin/activate
27	pip install -e .                     # installs the `league-table` command
28	pip install -r requirements-dev.txt  # pytest + coverage, for the tests
29	```
30	
31	## Usage
32	
33	Filenames, stdin/stdout, or any mixture of the two:
34	
35	```bash
36	league-table results.csv                     # file   -> stdout
37	league-table results.csv -o standings.csv    # file   -> file
38	cat results.csv | league-table               # stdin  -> stdout  (a plain unix filter)
39	league-table --pretty results.csv            # aligned text instead of CSV
40	python -m league_table results.csv           # without installing the console script
41	```
42	
43	**Input** needs four columns — `home_team`, `away_team`, `home_goals`, `away_goals` — in
44	any order. Any other column is ignored, which is how the shipped data files can carry
45	`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays.
46	
47	```csv
48	round,date,home_team,away_team,home_goals,away_goals
49	1,1974-08-17,Stoke City,Leeds United,3,0
50	```
51	
52	**Output** is the table in the conventional column order:
53	
54	```csv
55	Position,Team,Played,Won,Drawn,Lost,GoalsFor,GoalsAgainst,GoalAverage,Points
56	1,Ipswich Town,10,8,0,2,18,6,3.000,16
57	```
58	
59	Malformed input is rejected with a message on stderr naming the offending line. Nothing is
60	written to the output on failure — no half-written table, and an in-place rewrite
61	(`league-table results.csv -o results.csv`) is safe, because the table is rendered to memory
62	before the output is opened.
63	
64	| exit | meaning |
65	|---|---|
66	| 0 | the table was written |
67	| 2 | the input could not be read: bad CSV, missing/duplicated column, bad score, missing file |
68	| 141 | stdout was closed early (`\| head`), the conventional 128 + SIGPIPE |
69	
70	The parser rejects anything it cannot read *unambiguously* rather than guessing: a required
71	column named twice, a row with the wrong number of fields, a score that is not a plain run
72	of ASCII digits. (Python's own `int()` would accept `"1_0"` as 10 and non-ASCII digits as
73	numbers — either would silently put wrong goals in the table, which is worse than an error.)
74	Input is decoded as `utf-8-sig`, so the byte-order mark Excel and Google Sheets write by
75	default does not make the first column unreadable.
76	
77	## The rules, and why they are not the modern ones
78	
79	This is the crux of the exercise. Two rules differ from the modern game, and both were
80	verified against sources before the sort was written.
81	
82	### Two points for a win, one for a draw, none for a loss
83	
84	Three points for a win was **not** introduced in England until 1981/82. Wikipedia's
85	[1981–82 Football League][1981-82] page: *"This was the first league season with three
86	points for a win."* 1974/75 predates it, so a win is worth two.
87	
88	### Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE, not goal difference
89	
90	Goal average is a **ratio** — goals scored ÷ goals conceded. Goal difference (scored −
91	conceded) did not replace it in the Football League until 1976/77. Wikipedia's
92	[1974–75 Football League][1974-75] page:
93	
94	> Beginning with the season 1894–95, clubs finishing level on points were separated
95	> according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)... The goal average
96	> system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season.
97	
98	1974/75 sits squarely inside the goal-average era.
99	
100	**This is not a technicality — it changes the submitted table.** Middlesbrough and
101	Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points:
102	
103	| | scored | conceded | goal average | goal difference |
104	|---|---|---|---|---|
105	| Middlesbrough | 15 | 7 | **2.143** | +8 |
106	| Liverpool | 17 | 8 | 2.125 | **+9** |
107	
108	Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. The modern rule would swap them.
109	Lower down it is starker still: Tottenham, Arsenal and QPR all end matchday 10 on 6 points
110	*and* an identical −5 goal difference — the modern rule cannot separate them at all, while
111	goal average orders them 0.706 / 0.667 / 0.615.
112	
113	Both cases are locked down by tests in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
114	
115	### No further tie-break
116	
117	The Football League had none in 1974/75: clubs level on both points and goal average
118	genuinely shared the position. The tool falls back to alphabetical order purely so the
119	output is deterministic. That is a presentation choice, not a historical rule, and it is
120	marked as such in the code.
121	
122	## Goal average has two edge cases. Both are deliberate.
123	
124	A ratio has a denominator, so it can misbehave in ways a difference cannot:
125	
126	| case | arithmetic | decision | printed |
127	|---|---|---|---|
128	| Conceded nothing, scored something | division by zero | unbounded — ranks **above** every finite average | `inf` |
129	| Neither scored nor conceded (incl. a club with no games) | 0 ÷ 0 | undefined — ranks **below** every finite average | `n/a` |
130	
131	The reasoning: a club that has conceded nothing has an average better than any club that
132	has conceded, however many goals it scored — so it sorts top. A club with no goals either
133	way has no average at all; it has achieved nothing to be ranked *by*, so it cannot outrank
134	a club that has, and it sorts bottom. Note that 0 scored against 5 conceded is a **real**
135	average of 0.000, quite distinct from the undefined 0/0 case.
136	
137	The uncomfortable consequence, stated rather than hidden: an undefined club sorts below a
138	club with a genuine average of 0.000 — so a club that has *lost* can finish above a club
139	that is unbeaten, if they are level on points.
140	
141	```
142	Pos  Team         P  W  D  L  F  A   GAvg  Pts
143	  3  Beaten FC    2  0  1  1  0  2  0.000    1   <- lost a game
144	  4  Unbeaten FC  1  0  1  0  0  0    n/a    1   <- unbeaten, but 0/0
145	```
146	
147	Banding 0/0 with 0.000 instead does not rescue it: the two would then tie on average and
148	fall to the alphabetical tie-break, with Beaten FC still ahead. The case is genuinely
149	degenerate — it needs a goalless club, and it cannot arise in a league where every club has
150	scored or conceded at least once, as all 22 had by matchday 10 of 1974/75. We picked the
151	reading we could defend and wrote a test for it, rather than letting the sort decide by
152	accident.
153	
154	Averages are held as exact `Fraction`s, never floats — but the honest reason is narrower
155	than "floats would break it". At football scorelines they would not: a `float64` separates
156	14/11 from 25/19 with about fifteen digits to spare, and no club will ever score enough
157	goals to collide. `Fraction` is used so that the *correctness of the ordering does not
158	depend on the magnitude of the inputs at all* — the sort is exact by construction rather
159	than exact by luck, and nobody has to reason about ULPs to know the table is right. The
160	test that demonstrates the difference (`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_
161	float_rounding`) necessarily uses absurd goal counts to force a float collision, and is
162	labelled as the in-principle argument it is.
163	
164	## "The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous. Here is the reading, and the alternative.
165	
166	The season kicked off on Saturday 17 August 1974. The Football League squeezed extra
167	midweek rounds into the opening weeks (19–21 Aug, 27–28 Aug, 31 Aug), so the two natural
168	readings of "the 10th week" fall almost a month apart:
169	
170	- **After 10 matchdays** — every club has played 10 games, reached on Saturday 28 September 1974.
171	- **The 10th calendar week** — 19–25 October 1974, closing after Round 14 on Saturday the 19th,
172	  by which point clubs had played 12 to 14 games.
173	
174	**We use the matchday reading**, for three reasons:
175	
176	1. It is the ordinary football usage. "Week 10", "gameweek 10" and "matchweek 10" all mean
177	   *ten rounds played*. Nobody describes the 19 October 1974 table as "the 10th week".
178	2. The calendar reading is not even well-defined. Is a week Saturday-to-Friday from kickoff,
179	   or Monday-to-Sunday? Moving the boundary two days moves the answer from round 13 to round
180	   14 — a different table. A rule that fragile cannot be the intended one.
181	3. It is the reading under which every club has played the same number of games, so the
182	   table can be read straight down without mentally adjusting for games in hand.
183	
184	That third point is a convenience, not a principle — real First Division tables were
185	published mid-week with clubs on unequal games played all the time, and the 11v11 table
186	cited below is exactly that. It is a reason to *lead* with the matchday table, not a reason
187	the other one is wrong, which is why both are shipped.
188	
189	### Both cut-offs are shipped
190	
191	Within the matchday reading there is one further wrinkle worth being honest about. Three
192	round-9 fixtures were postponed and actually played months later — Leeds v Tottenham
193	(4 Dec 1974), Middlesbrough v Leicester (10 Dec 1974) and Newcastle v Arsenal (23 Apr 1975).
194	A "rounds 1–10" table therefore includes three results that were not on the pitch in
195	September. So both readings ship:
196	
197	### All three readings are shipped
198	
199	Rather than argue the ambiguity and pick one in private, every reading is in the repo as an
200	input/output pair. The same binary produces all three — only the input differs, which is the
201	whole point of keeping the week out of the code.
202	
203	| input | output | what it is |
204	|---|---|---|
205	| `results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` (110 matches) | `standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` | **The submitted answer.** Ten matchdays complete; all clubs P=10. |
206	| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv` (107 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` | The table as it actually stood on matchday 10's evening: six clubs on P=9. |
207	| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv` (148 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv` | The **10th calendar week**, ending Sat 19 Oct 1974. Clubs on 12–14 games. |
208	
209	The honest tension, since it cuts against the file we lead with: the matchday-10 table
210	contains three results that had not been played in September — one of them not until April
211	1975. It is a *reconstruction* of a ten-game table, not a table anyone could have printed at
212	the time. We lead with it because "the 10th week" most naturally means ten rounds played,
213	and because it is the table the archives reproduce — but a reviewer who reads the brief the
214	other way will find their table here too, computed by the same code.
215	
216	### The calendar-week table is also the strongest evidence the tool is right
217	
218	The 19 October 1974 table is worth more than completeness. [11v11 publishes the table for
219	that exact date][11v11-19oct], and — unusually — prints its own **goal average** column and
220	orders the clubs by it. Our output reproduces it **exactly: all 22 rows, in the same order,
221	with the same goal averages to three decimal places.**
222	
223	```
224	Pos  Team                P   W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
225	  1  Liverpool           13  9  1  3  21   8  2.625   19     <- 13 games
226	  2  Manchester City     14  8  3  3  19  15  1.267   19     <- 14 games
227	  3  Ipswich Town        14  8  1  5  18   9  2.000   17
228	  4  Everton             14  4  9  1  19  16  1.188   17
229	  ...
230	 18  Leicester City      12  3  4  5  16  18  0.889   10     <- 12 games
231	```
232	
233	That single comparison exercises everything at once — the match data, two points for a win,
234	the goal-average *ratio*, the ordering, and clubs on unequal games played (six postponed
235	fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13). Note Liverpool top the table on 19
236	points from **thirteen** games while Manchester City have 19 from **fourteen**: separated by
237	goal average, 2.625 to 1.267. If any part of the implementation were wrong, that table could
238	not come out right. It is asserted row by row in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
239	
240	(One thing we do *not* claim: in the two cases where clubs tie on both points and goal
241	average — Burnley/Newcastle on 1.000, Birmingham/Wolverhampton on 1.000 — our alphabetical
242	fallback happens to agree with 11v11's order. But goals-scored would order both pairs the
243	same way, so this data cannot tell us which rule 11v11 applies, and we do not pretend it
244	does.)
245	
246	## Tests
247	
248	```bash
249	pytest
250	```
251	
252	99 tests. The suite enforces **100% branch coverage** and fails below it; the only excluded
253	line in the codebase is the `if __name__ == "__main__":` process entry point, which cannot
254	be exercised in-process.
255	
256	Coverage is a floor, not a goal — and it is not enough on its own. A QA review of this repo
257	found the `--pretty` formatter was *executed* by the tests but never *asserted on*: swapping
258	its `ljust`/`rjust` wrecked the layout and every test still passed. It is now pinned
259	character for character. Several real bugs hid behind a green 100% bar (a duplicated CSV
260	column silently producing wrong numbers; a BOM making a valid file unreadable; wrong line
261	numbers in errors; a `BrokenPipeError` traceback when piped to `head`). All are fixed, and
262	each has a test that fails without the fix.
263	
264	The tests that actually protect this submission are:
265	
266	- `test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree` — two clubs the historical rule and the
267	  modern rule order **differently**. It asserts the disagreement explicitly, so it cannot
268	  pass by coincidence. If someone "modernises" the sort, this is what fails.
269	- `test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference` — the same
270	  thing, in the real season, on the real submitted table.
271	- `test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly` — all 22 rows of
272	  a **published historical table**, reproduced exactly: same order, same goal averages, clubs
273	  on unequal games played. The single strongest check in the repo.
274	- `test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding` — fails under a float
275	  implementation.
276	- The goal-average edge cases, the CSV failure modes and exit codes, and a golden test
277	  pinning the committed output files to what the tool produces today.
278	- The adversarial cases a review turned up: a duplicated header column, a row wider than its

# League Standings — English First Division, 1974/75

A command-line tool that reads football results from CSV and prints a league standing
table, using the rules in force in the English First Division in the 1974/75 season.

The tool is general: it tabulates whatever results it is given. It knows nothing about
weeks, matchdays or 1974 — those live in the data, not the code.

```
$ league-table --pretty data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv
Pos  Team                      P  W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
  1  Ipswich Town             10  8  0  2  18   6  3.000   16
  2  Newcastle United         10  6  2  2  19  14  1.357   14
  3  Manchester City          10  6  2  2  14  11  1.273   14
  4  Middlesbrough            10  5  3  2  15   7  2.143   13
  5  Liverpool                10  6  1  3  17   8  2.125   13
  ...
```

## Setup

Python 3.9+. No runtime dependencies; the tool itself is pure standard library.

```bash
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e .                     # installs the `league-table` command
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt  # pytest + coverage, for the tests
```

## Usage

Filenames, stdin/stdout, or any mixture of the two:

```bash
league-table results.csv                     # file   -> stdout
league-table results.csv -o standings.csv    # file   -> file
cat results.csv | league-table               # stdin  -> stdout  (a plain unix filter)
league-table --pretty results.csv            # aligned text instead of CSV
python -m league_table results.csv           # without installing the console script
```

**Input** needs four columns — `home_team`, `away_team`, `home_goals`, `away_goals` — in
any order. Any other column is ignored, which is how the shipped data files can carry
`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays.

```csv
round,date,home_team,away_team,home_goals,away_goals
1,1974-08-17,Stoke City,Leeds United,3,0
```

**Output** is the table in the conventional column order:

```csv
Position,Team,Played,Won,Drawn,Lost,GoalsFor,GoalsAgainst,GoalAverage,Points
1,Ipswich Town,10,8,0,2,18,6,3.000,16
```

Malformed input is rejected with a message on stderr naming the offending line. Nothing is
written to the output on failure — no half-written table, and an in-place rewrite
(`league-table results.csv -o results.csv`) is safe, because the table is rendered to memory
before the output is opened.

| exit | meaning |
|---|---|
| 0 | the table was written |
| 2 | the input could not be read: bad CSV, missing/duplicated column, bad score, missing file |
| 141 | stdout was closed early (`\| head`), the conventional 128 + SIGPIPE |

The parser rejects anything it cannot read *unambiguously* rather than guessing: a required
column named twice, a row with the wrong number of fields, a score that is not a plain run
of ASCII digits. (Python's own `int()` would accept `"1_0"` as 10 and non-ASCII digits as
numbers — either would silently put wrong goals in the table, which is worse than an error.)
Input is decoded as `utf-8-sig`, so the byte-order mark Excel and Google Sheets write by
default does not make the first column unreadable.

## The rules, and why they are not the modern ones

This is the crux of the exercise. Two rules differ from the modern game, and both were
verified against sources before the sort was written.

### Two points for a win, one for a draw, none for a loss

Three points for a win was **not** introduced in England until 1981/82. Wikipedia's
[1981–82 Football League][1981-82] page: *"This was the first league season with three
points for a win."* 1974/75 predates it, so a win is worth two.

### Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE, not goal difference

Goal average is a **ratio** — goals scored ÷ goals conceded. Goal difference (scored −
conceded) did not replace it in the Football League until 1976/77. Wikipedia's
[1974–75 Football League][1974-75] page:

> Beginning with the season 1894–95, clubs finishing level on points were separated
> according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)... The goal average
> system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season.

1974/75 sits squarely inside the goal-average era.

**This is not a technicality — it changes the submitted table.** Middlesbrough and
Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points:

| | scored | conceded | goal average | goal difference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Middlesbrough | 15 | 7 | **2.143** | +8 |
| Liverpool | 17 | 8 | 2.125 | **+9** |

Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. The modern rule would swap them.
Lower down it is starker still: Tottenham, Arsenal and QPR all end matchday 10 on 6 points
*and* an identical −5 goal difference — the modern rule cannot separate them at all, while
goal average orders them 0.706 / 0.667 / 0.615.

Both cases are locked down by tests in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.

### No further tie-break

The Football League had none in 1974/75: clubs level on both points and goal average
genuinely shared the position. The tool falls back to alphabetical order purely so the
output is deterministic. That is a presentation choice, not a historical rule, and it is
marked as such in the code.

## Goal average has two edge cases. Both are deliberate.

A ratio has a denominator, so it can misbehave in ways a difference cannot:

| case | arithmetic | decision | printed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conceded nothing, scored something | division by zero | unbounded — ranks **above** every finite average | `inf` |
| Neither scored nor conceded (incl. a club with no games) | 0 ÷ 0 | undefined — ranks **below** every finite average | `n/a` |

The reasoning: a club that has conceded nothing has an average better than any club that
has conceded, however many goals it scored — so it sorts top. A club with no goals either
way has no average at all; it has achieved nothing to be ranked *by*, so it cannot outrank
a club that has, and it sorts bottom. Note that 0 scored against 5 conceded is a **real**
average of 0.000, quite distinct from the undefined 0/0 case.

The uncomfortable consequence, stated rather than hidden: an undefined club sorts below a
club with a genuine average of 0.000 — so a club that has *lost* can finish above a club
that is unbeaten, if they are level on points.

```
Pos  Team         P  W  D  L  F  A   GAvg  Pts
  3  Beaten FC    2  0  1  1  0  2  0.000    1   <- lost a game
  4  Unbeaten FC  1  0  1  0  0  0    n/a    1   <- unbeaten, but 0/0
```

Banding 0/0 with 0.000 instead does not rescue it: the two would then tie on average and
fall to the alphabetical tie-break, with Beaten FC still ahead. The case is genuinely
degenerate — it needs a goalless club, and it cannot arise in a league where every club has
scored or conceded at least once, as all 22 had by matchday 10 of 1974/75. We picked the
reading we could defend and wrote a test for it, rather than letting the sort decide by
accident.

Averages are held as exact `Fraction`s, never floats — but the honest reason is narrower
than "floats would break it". At football scorelines they would not: a `float64` separates
14/11 from 25/19 with about fifteen digits to spare, and no club will ever score enough
goals to collide. `Fraction` is used so that the *correctness of the ordering does not
depend on the magnitude of the inputs at all* — the sort is exact by construction rather
than exact by luck, and nobody has to reason about ULPs to know the table is right. The
test that demonstrates the difference (`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_
float_rounding`) necessarily uses absurd goal counts to force a float collision, and is
labelled as the in-principle argument it is.

## "The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous. Here is the reading, and the alternative.

The season kicked off on Saturday 17 August 1974. The Football League squeezed extra
midweek rounds into the opening weeks (19–21 Aug, 27–28 Aug, 31 Aug), so the two natural
readings of "the 10th week" fall almost a month apart:

- **After 10 matchdays** — every club has played 10 games, reached on Saturday 28 September 1974.
- **The 10th calendar week** — 19–25 October 1974, closing after Round 14 on Saturday the 19th,
  by which point clubs had played 12 to 14 games.

**We use the matchday reading**, for three reasons:

1. It is the ordinary football usage. "Week 10", "gameweek 10" and "matchweek 10" all mean
   *ten rounds played*. Nobody describes the 19 October 1974 table as "the 10th week".
2. The calendar reading is not even well-defined. Is a week Saturday-to-Friday from kickoff,
   or Monday-to-Sunday? Moving the boundary two days moves the answer from round 13 to round
   14 — a different table. A rule that fragile cannot be the intended one.
3. It is the reading under which every club has played the same number of games, so the
   table can be read straight down without mentally adjusting for games in hand.

That third point is a convenience, not a principle — real First Division tables were
published mid-week with clubs on unequal games played all the time, and the 11v11 table
cited below is exactly that. It is a reason to *lead* with the matchday table, not a reason
the other one is wrong, which is why both are shipped.

### Both cut-offs are shipped

Within the matchday reading there is one further wrinkle worth being honest about. Three
round-9 fixtures were postponed and actually played months later — Leeds v Tottenham
(4 Dec 1974), Middlesbrough v Leicester (10 Dec 1974) and Newcastle v Arsenal (23 Apr 1975).
A "rounds 1–10" table therefore includes three results that were not on the pitch in
September. So both readings ship:

### All three readings are shipped

Rather than argue the ambiguity and pick one in private, every reading is in the repo as an
input/output pair. The same binary produces all three — only the input differs, which is the
whole point of keeping the week out of the code.

| input | output | what it is |
|---|---|---|
| `results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` (110 matches) | `standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` | **The submitted answer.** Ten matchdays complete; all clubs P=10. |
| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv` (107 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` | The table as it actually stood on matchday 10's evening: six clubs on P=9. |
| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv` (148 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv` | The **10th calendar week**, ending Sat 19 Oct 1974. Clubs on 12–14 games. |

The honest tension, since it cuts against the file we lead with: the matchday-10 table
contains three results that had not been played in September — one of them not until April
1975. It is a *reconstruction* of a ten-game table, not a table anyone could have printed at
the time. We lead with it because "the 10th week" most naturally means ten rounds played,
and because it is the table the archives reproduce — but a reviewer who reads the brief the
other way will find their table here too, computed by the same code.

### The calendar-week table is also the strongest evidence the tool is right

The 19 October 1974 table is worth more than completeness. [11v11 publishes the table for
that exact date][11v11-19oct], and — unusually — prints its own **goal average** column and
orders the clubs by it. Our output reproduces it **exactly: all 22 rows, in the same order,
with the same goal averages to three decimal places.**

```
Pos  Team                P   W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
  1  Liverpool           13  9  1  3  21   8  2.625   19     <- 13 games
  2  Manchester City     14  8  3  3  19  15  1.267   19     <- 14 games
  3  Ipswich Town        14  8  1  5  18   9  2.000   17
  4  Everton             14  4  9  1  19  16  1.188   17
  ...
 18  Leicester City      12  3  4  5  16  18  0.889   10     <- 12 games
```

That single comparison exercises everything at once — the match data, two points for a win,
the goal-average *ratio*, the ordering, and clubs on unequal games played (six postponed
fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13). Note Liverpool top the table on 19
points from **thirteen** games while Manchester City have 19 from **fourteen**: separated by
goal average, 2.625 to 1.267. If any part of the implementation were wrong, that table could
not come out right. It is asserted row by row in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.

(One thing we do *not* claim: in the two cases where clubs tie on both points and goal
average — Burnley/Newcastle on 1.000, Birmingham/Wolverhampton on 1.000 — our alphabetical
fallback happens to agree with 11v11's order. But goals-scored would order both pairs the
same way, so this data cannot tell us which rule 11v11 applies, and we do not pretend it
does.)

## Tests

```bash
pytest
```

99 tests. The suite enforces **100% branch coverage** and fails below it; the only excluded
line in the codebase is the `if __name__ == "__main__":` process entry point, which cannot
be exercised in-process.

Coverage is a floor, not a goal — and it is not enough on its own. A QA review of this repo
found the `--pretty` formatter was *executed* by the tests but never *asserted on*: swapping
its `ljust`/`rjust` wrecked the layout and every test still passed. It is now pinned
character for character. Several real bugs hid behind a green 100% bar (a duplicated CSV
column silently producing wrong numbers; a BOM making a valid file unreadable; wrong line
numbers in errors; a `BrokenPipeError` traceback when piped to `head`). All are fixed, and
each has a test that fails without the fix.

The tests that actually protect this submission are:

- `test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree` — two clubs the historical rule and the
  modern rule order **differently**. It asserts the disagreement explicitly, so it cannot
  pass by coincidence. If someone "modernises" the sort, this is what fails.
- `test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference` — the same
  thing, in the real season, on the real submitted table.
- `test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly` — all 22 rows of
  a **published historical table**, reproduced exactly: same order, same goal averages, clubs
  on unequal games played. The single strongest check in the repo.
- `test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding` — fails under a float
  implementation.
- The goal-average edge cases, the CSV failure modes and exit codes, and a golden test
  pinning the committed output files to what the tool produces today.
- The adversarial cases a review turned up: a duplicated header column, a row wider than its
  header, a BOM, a non-UTF-8 file, a club name containing a newline (which breaks naive line
  counting), scores like `"1_0"` that `int()` would silently accept, and a closed pipe.

## Design

```
src/league_table/
  rules.py    # the 1974/75 rules: points, goal average, ordering. Pure; no I/O.
  models.py   # Match, Standing
  table.py    # results -> standing table. Pure; no I/O.
  csvio.py    # all the CSV parsing and writing
  cli.py      # argument parsing and streams
```

The domain (`rules.py`, `table.py`, `models.py`) never touches the filesystem, so the
historical rules are unit-testable without a CSV in sight — and the rules are stated once,
in one file, where they can be checked against a source.

`Standing` deliberately has **no goal-difference column**. The 1974/75 table was published
with goals for, goals against and goal average; printing a difference beside them would
invite exactly the confusion this exercise is about.

## Data provenance

Match results were taken from [worldfootball.net's 1974/75 fixture archive][worldfootball]
(rounds 1–14, 154 matches) and validated on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22
clubs appearing exactly once per round, and the club list an exact set-match with the 22
clubs in Wikipedia's [final 1974–75 First Division table][1974-75]. Every match carries the
date it was **actually played**, not its nominal matchday — six fixtures were postponed
(three from round 9, three from round 13) and played as late as April 1975, which is what
makes the date-based cut-offs meaningful.

The computed table was then checked against two independent sources:

- **[worldfootball.net's matchday-10 table][wf-md10]** (all 22 clubs on P=10): every club
  agrees on played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals against and points. Its *row order*
  differs from ours — it shows Liverpool 7th where we have them 4th — but we deliberately
  do not lean on its ordering, only its numbers. (Most of its points-blocks are ordered by
  goal difference, but the 13-point block matches neither goal difference nor goal average,
  so we make no claim about what rule it applies.)
- **[11v11's table for 28 September 1974][11v11]** (date-based, so six clubs show P=9): all
  sixteen clubs on P=10 agree exactly, and 11v11's own goal-average column matches ours to
  three decimal places. The six P=9 clubs differ from ours by precisely one result each,
  with no leftover goals or points — those deltas reconstruct exactly the three postponed
  round-9 fixtures listed above, so the two datasets corroborate one another.

[1974-75]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Football_League
[1981-82]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Football_League
[worldfootball]: https://www.worldfootball.net/all_matches/eng-premier-league-1974-1975/
[wf-md10]: https://www.worldfootball.net/schedule/eng-premier-league-1974-1975-spieltag/10/

[11v11-19oct]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/

Domain: the 1974/75 rules, and a standing table built from them
Two points for a win, one for a draw; clubs level on points separated by goal
AVERAGE (goals scored / goals conceded), not goal difference. Both rules were
verified against cited sources before this sort was written -- goal difference
did not replace goal average in the Football League until 1976/77, and three
points for a win arrived in 1981/82. Sources are cited in README.md.

Goal average is a Fraction, never a float, so the ordering is exact by
construction rather than by luck. Its two degenerate cases are deliberate: a
club that has conceded nothing ranks above every finite average; a club with no
goals either way (0/0) has no average at all and ranks below them.

The load-bearing test is test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree: two
clubs the historical rule and the modern rule order OPPOSITELY. It asserts the
disagreement explicitly, so it cannot pass by coincidence, and anyone who
"modernises" the sort gets a red test instead of a plausible table.

Pure: no I/O anywhere in this layer.

c63873d664814391dd6bf4bb71e2ca55395e33d8
The 1974/75 answer: match data, and all three readings of "the 10th week"
Results scraped from worldfootball.net (rounds 1-14, 154 matches) and validated
on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22 clubs appearing exactly once
per round, club list an exact set-match with Wikipedia's final 1974-75 table.
Every match carries the date it was ACTUALLY played -- six fixtures were
postponed and played as late as April 1975 -- which is what makes the date-based
cut-offs below meaningful.

"The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous, because the League played extra
midweek rounds early on: ten matchdays were complete by 28 September 1974, only
six calendar weeks in. Rather than pick a reading in private, all three ship, and
the same binary produces each from a different input:

  rounds 1-10          -> the submitted answer; all clubs P=10
  played by 28 Sep 74  -> the table as it actually stood; six clubs on P=9
  played by 19 Oct 74  -> the literal 10th calendar week; clubs on 12-14 games

The last of these is also the strongest check in the repo. 11v11 publishes the
table for 19 October 1974 with its own goal-average column, ordered by it, and
our output reproduces it EXACTLY: all 22 rows, same order, same averages to three
decimals, clubs on unequal games played. Liverpool top it on 19 points from 13
games, ahead of Manchester City on 19 from 14 -- separated by goal average, 2.625
to 1.267. If the data, the points rule, the ratio or the sort were wrong, that
table could not come out right.

I/O: CSV parsing, and a CLI that behaves like a unix filter
Filenames or stdin/stdout in any combination. The table is rendered to memory
before the output is opened, so a malformed input never leaves a half-written
table and an in-place rewrite is safe.

The parser needs four columns and ignores every other one, which is what keeps
the tool general: the data files carry `round` and `date` for a human's benefit
while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays and seasons. It refuses anything it
cannot read unambiguously rather than guessing -- a duplicated required column,
a row of the wrong width, or a score that is not a plain run of ASCII digits
(int() would silently accept "1_0" as 10). Bad input exits 2 with the offending
line number; a closed pipe exits 141 rather than dumping a traceback.

# AI Reflection

## The coverage gate made the AI delete a bug into existence

I asked for TDD and a 100% branch coverage gate. Those two things together caused the most
interesting failure of the session.

Partway through the sort, the coverage report flagged a method as never executed. Claude
reasoned that nothing would ever call it, concluded it was dead, and deleted it. The reasoning
sounded fine and was wrong. That method was quietly holding up the tie-break between clubs
level on points and goal average, and without it the order of those clubs became arbitrary.
Nothing crashed. The table just would have been slightly wrong in a way nobody would ever have
noticed.

The test I'd written first caught it in about ten seconds, which is the whole argument for TDD
in one incident. If we'd written the code first, I'm fairly sure the test would have been
written to match whatever the code did, and it would have shipped green.

What I took from it is narrower than "TDD good". An uncovered line is a question, not an
answer. It tells you no test touches this, which is just as consistent with "this is
load-bearing and nobody tested it" as with "this is dead". Claude resolved that ambiguity in
the direction that made the number go up, which is what you'd expect from anything you hand a
target to. I stopped treating the coverage gate as permission to delete code and started
treating it as a list of things to go and test.

A smaller version happened twice, the other way round: Claude wrote a test, the code disagreed,
and the code was right. Writing the test first doesn't make it correct. It just means you find
out early.

## Pushing back on "the 10th week"

Claude's first instinct was to read "the 10th week of the season" as ten matchdays and get on
with it. I didn't buy it, because that only works if fixtures land one per week, and I doubted
they had. They hadn't. The League played extra midweek rounds early on, so every club had ten
games in by late September, while the literal tenth calendar week doesn't arrive until the
middle of October. The two readings are almost a month apart.

I went with the matchday reading, but the decision that mattered was structural. The tool
doesn't know what a week is. It tabulates whatever file you give it, and the cut-off lives in
the data. So all three readings cost nothing to ship, and all three ship. A judgement call I
could have got wrong became one I didn't have to make irreversibly.

The calendar-week file turned out to be the most useful thing in the repo, because 11v11
publishes the real table for that date with its own goal average column, and ours reproduces it
exactly. That single comparison tests the data, the rules and the sort all at once.

## The part I'm least pleased about

I made Claude verify the historical rules against real sources before writing the sort, and
cross-check the scraped results against two archives rather than trusting the scrape. The rules
are the trap in this exercise: a beautifully tested implementation of goal difference is a wrong
answer, and it's wrong silently.

Then I fell for exactly the thing I'd been guarding against.

An agent told me worldfootball sorts its table by goal difference, which would neatly explain
why the two tables disagree about Liverpool. Nice story. The old rule, visible in the wild, on
the very season the exercise is about. I liked it enough to put it in the README and in this
document, and I never opened the page. A later review did, and it's false. Whatever explains
their row order, it isn't that, and the page I cited disproves it.

The data cross-check itself held up, which is what it was actually for. What didn't hold up was
my explanation of somebody else's table, taken from a model's summary because it was pleasing
and it confirmed the point I wanted to make. I deleted the claim rather than patch it. I don't
know what worldfootball sorts by and I don't need to.

So the lesson is smaller and more annoying than "verify things". A model's summary of a source
is not the source, and the more a claim flatters your argument, the more it deserves the click.
The match data got that scrutiny and survived. My favourite sentence in the write-up didn't,
and was wrong.

    64	    # both would silently produce a wrong table rather than an error.

    82	def read_matches(stream: IO[str]) -> List[Match]:
    83	    """Parse match results from an open CSV stream.

   101	            + ", ".join(missing)

   103	        )

   106	    # would win silently, and the table would be quietly wrong. Refuse instead.
   107	    duplicated = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if seen[column] > 1]

   111	            + ", ".join(duplicated)

   139	            Match(
   140	                home_team=home,
   141	                away_team=away,
   142	                home_goals=_read_goals(row, "home_goals", line),
   143	                away_goals=_read_goals(row, "away_goals", line),
   144	            )
   145	        )

     1	"""The scoring and ranking rules of the English First Division, 1974/75.
     2	
     3	These are historical rules, not modern ones. See README.md for the sources.
     4	
     5	Two rules matter and both differ from the modern game:
     6	
     7	* Two points for a win, one for a draw. Three points for a win was not
     8	  introduced in England until 1981/82.
     9	* Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE (goals scored divided by
    10	  goals conceded), not goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal
    11	  average in the Football League until 1976/77.
    12	
    13	This module is pure: no I/O, no CSV, no filesystem.
    14	"""
    15	
    16	import functools
    17	from fractions import Fraction
    18	from typing import Optional, Tuple
    19	
    20	POINTS_FOR_WIN = 2
    21	POINTS_FOR_DRAW = 1
    22	POINTS_FOR_LOSS = 0
    23	
    24	
    25	def points_for_result(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> int:
    26	    """Points a club earns from a single match with this scoreline."""
    27	    if goals_for > goals_against:
    28	        return POINTS_FOR_WIN
    29	    if goals_for == goals_against:
    30	        return POINTS_FOR_DRAW
    31	    return POINTS_FOR_LOSS
    32	
    33	
    34	# Ranking bands for the two degenerate cases of a ratio. A club that has
    35	# conceded nothing has an unbounded average and so outranks every finite one; a
    36	# club with no goals either way (including one that has played no games) has no
    37	# average at all -- 0/0 -- and cannot outrank a club that achieved a real ratio.
    38	# Both are deliberate choices, not accidents of arithmetic; see README.md.
    39	_UNDEFINED = 0
    40	_FINITE = 1
    41	_INFINITE = 2
    42	
    43	
    44	@functools.total_ordering
    45	class GoalAverage:
    46	    """Goals scored divided by goals conceded, as a totally ordered value.
    47	
    48	    Held as a `Fraction`, never a float: averages like 14/11 and 25/19 are
    49	    compared directly against one another, and float rounding would silently
    50	    equate values that differ, mis-ordering the table.
    51	
    52	    Greater is better, so these objects sort the way the league table reads.
    53	    """
    54	
    55	    __slots__ = ("_band", "_value")
    56	
    57	    def __init__(self, goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> None:
    58	        if goals_against > 0:
    59	            self._band = _FINITE
    60	            self._value: Optional[Fraction] = Fraction(goals_for, goals_against)
    61	        elif goals_for > 0:
    62	            self._band = _INFINITE
    63	            self._value = None
    64	        else:
    65	            self._band = _UNDEFINED
    66	            self._value = None
    67	
    68	    @property
    69	    def value(self) -> Optional[Fraction]:
    70	        """The exact ratio, or None where it is infinite or undefined."""
    71	        return self._value
    72	
    73	    @property
    74	    def is_infinite(self) -> bool:
    75	        """True when the club has scored but conceded nothing."""
    76	        return self._band == _INFINITE
    77	
    78	    @property
    79	    def is_undefined(self) -> bool:
    80	        """True when the club has neither scored nor conceded (0/0)."""
    81	        return self._band == _UNDEFINED
    82	
    83	    def sort_key(self) -> Tuple[int, Fraction]:
    84	        """A plain (band, ratio) pair. Higher is better, in both elements.
    85	
    86	        Both numbers negate cleanly, so a caller wanting descending order can
    87	        simply negate them rather than wrap this in a reversing adapter.
    88	        """
    89	        ratio = Fraction(0) if self._value is None else self._value
    90	        return (self._band, ratio)
    91

    93	        if not isinstance(other, GoalAverage):
    94	            return NotImplemented

    96	
    97	    def __lt__(self, other: "GoalAverage") -> bool:
    98	        if not isinstance(other, GoalAverage):
    99	            return NotImplemented

   101	
   102	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   103	        return hash(self.sort_key())
   104	
   105	    def __str__(self) -> str:
   106	        if self._band == _INFINITE:
   107	            return "inf"
   108	        if self._band == _UNDEFINED:
   109	            return "n/a"
   110	        return f"{float(self._value):.3f}"
   111	
   112	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
   113	        return f"GoalAverage({self})"
   114	
   115	
   116	def goal_average(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> GoalAverage:
   117	    """The 1974/75 tie-break: goals scored divided by goals conceded.
   118	
   119	    NOT goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal average in the
   120	    Football League until 1976/77 and is the wrong rule for this season.
   121	    """
   122	    return GoalAverage(goals_for, goals_against)

     1	"""Turning a list of match results into a standing table.
     2	
     3	Pure domain: no I/O. The rules it applies live in `rules.py`.
     4	
     5	The tool knows nothing about weeks, matchdays or the 1974/75 season. It
     6	tabulates whatever results it is given; deciding *which* results constitute
     7	"the 10th week" is the caller's business, and is discussed in README.md.
     8	"""
     9	
    10	from typing import Dict, Iterable, List, Tuple
    11	
    12	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    13	from league_table.rules import goal_average, points_for_result
    14	
    15	
    16	class _Tally:
    17	    """A club's running totals while the results are being read."""
    18	
    19	    __slots__ = ("won", "drawn", "lost", "goals_for", "goals_against", "points")
    20	
    21	    def __init__(self) -> None:
    22	        self.won = 0
    23	        self.drawn = 0
    24	        self.lost = 0
    25	        self.goals_for = 0
    26	        self.goals_against = 0
    27	        self.points = 0
    28	
    29	    def record(self, scored: int, conceded: int) -> None:
    30	        self.goals_for += scored
    31	        self.goals_against += conceded
    32	        self.points += points_for_result(scored, conceded)
    33	
    34	        if scored > conceded:
    35	            self.won += 1
    36	        elif scored == conceded:
    37	            self.drawn += 1
    38	        else:
    39	            self.lost += 1
    40	
    41	    @property
    42	    def played(self) -> int:
    43	        return self.won + self.drawn + self.lost
    44	
    45	
    46	def _ranking_key(entry: Tuple[str, _Tally]):
    47	    """The 1974/75 order: points, then goal average, then club name.
    48	
    49	    Points and the goal average both sort descending -- higher is better -- and
    50	    both are plain numbers, so they are simply negated. The club name breaks the
    51	    remaining ties ascending.
    52	
    53	    The name is not a historical rule. The Football League had no tie-break
    54	    beyond goal average; clubs level on both genuinely shared the position. It is
    55	    here so the output is deterministic, and it is documented as such.
    56	    """
    57	    team, tally = entry
    58	    band, ratio = goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against).sort_key()
    59	    return (-tally.points, -band, -ratio, team)
    60	
    61	
    62	def build_table(matches: Iterable[Match]) -> List[Standing]:
    63	    """The standing table for these results, best club first."""
    64	    tallies: Dict[str, _Tally] = {}
    65	
    66	    for match in matches:
    67	        home = tallies.setdefault(match.home_team, _Tally())
    68	        away = tallies.setdefault(match.away_team, _Tally())
    69	
    70	        home.record(match.home_goals, match.away_goals)
    71	        away.record(match.away_goals, match.home_goals)
    72	
    73	    ranked = sorted(tallies.items(), key=_ranking_key)
    74	
    75	    return [
    76	        Standing(
    77	            position=position,
    78	            team=team,
    79	            played=tally.played,
    80	            won=tally.won,
    81	            drawn=tally.drawn,
    82	            lost=tally.lost,
    83	            goals_for=tally.goals_for,
    84	            goals_against=tally.goals_against,
    85	            goal_average=goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against),
    86	            points=tally.points,
    87	        )
    88	        for position, (team, tally) in enumerate(ranked, start=1)
    89	    ]

    38	def _normalise_header(fieldnames: Sequence[str]) -> List[str]:
    39	    # A leading BOM would otherwise ride along on the first column name, so that
    40	    # 'home_team' fails to match 'home_team'. Files opened by this module are
    41	    # decoded as utf-8-sig, but a caller can hand us any stream -- stdin, most
    42	    # obviously -- so strip it here too.

    47	    """True for a plain run of ASCII digits, and nothing else."""
    48	    return value.isascii() and value.isdigit()

    51	def _read_goals(row: dict, column: str, line: int) -> int:
    52	    # Every required column is guaranteed present: the header is checked once up
    53	    # front, and rows of the wrong width are rejected before we get here.
    54	    value = row[column].strip()

    56	    if value == "":
    57	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: missing a value for '{column}'")
    58	
    59	    if value.startswith("-") and _is_goal_count(value[1:]):
    60	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: '{column}' cannot be negative, got {value}")
    61	
    62	    # Deliberately stricter than int(), which accepts PEP 515 underscores
    63	    # ("1_0" -> 10) and non-ASCII decimal digits. Neither is a goal count, and
    64	    # both would silently produce a wrong table rather than an error.
    65	    if not _is_goal_count(value):
    66	        raise InvalidInput(
    67	            f"line {line}: '{column}' must be a whole number of goals, got {value!r}"
    68	        )
    69	
    70	    return int(value)

    74	    name = row[column].strip()
    75	
    76	    if name == "":
    77	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: '{column}' is blank; every club needs a name")
    78	
    79	    return name

    82	def read_matches(stream: IO[str]) -> List[Match]:
    83	    """Parse match results from an open CSV stream.
    84	
    85	    Raises `InvalidInput` -- with the line number -- on anything it cannot read.
    86	    """
    87	    reader = csv.reader(stream)
    88	
    89	    try:
    90	        raw_header = next(reader)
    91	    except StopIteration:
    92	        raise InvalidInput("the input is empty: no header row found") from None
    93	
    94	    header = _normalise_header(raw_header)
    95	    seen = Counter(header)
    96	
    97	    missing = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if column not in header]
    98	    if missing:
    99	        raise InvalidInput(
   100	            "the header is missing required column(s): "
   101	            + ", ".join(missing)
   102	            + f". Expected at least: {', '.join(REQUIRED_COLUMNS)}"
   103	        )
   104	
   105	    # A required column appearing twice cannot be resolved: one of the two values
   106	    # would win silently, and the table would be quietly wrong. Refuse instead.
   107	    duplicated = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if seen[column] > 1]
   108	    if duplicated:
   109	        raise InvalidInput(
   110	            "the header names these required column(s) more than once: "
   111	            + ", ".join(duplicated)
   112	        )

   114	    matches = []
   115	
   116	    for values in reader:
   117	        # csv records and physical lines are not the same thing: a quoted club
   118	        # name may span lines. `line_num` is the reader's own count of physical
   119	        # lines, which is the number a human needs in order to find the row.
   120	        line = reader.line_num

   122	        if not any(value.strip() for value in values):
   123	            continue  # a blank separator line is not an error

   125	        if len(values) != len(header):

   127	                f"line {line}: expected {len(header)} columns, found {len(values)}"

   133	        away = _read_team(row, "away_team", line)

   135	        if home == away:

   138	        matches.append(
   139	            Match(
   140	                home_team=home,
   141	                away_team=away,
   142	                home_goals=_read_goals(row, "home_goals", line),
   143	                away_goals=_read_goals(row, "away_goals", line),
   144	            )
   145	        )

   147	    return matches

   162	                row.drawn,
   163	                row.lost,

     1	"""The scoring and ranking rules of the English First Division, 1974/75.
     2	
     3	These are historical rules, not modern ones. See README.md for the sources.
     4	
     5	Two rules matter and both differ from the modern game:
     6	
     7	* Two points for a win, one for a draw. Three points for a win was not
     8	  introduced in England until 1981/82.
     9	* Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE (goals scored divided by
    10	  goals conceded), not goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal
    11	  average in the Football League until 1976/77.
    12	
    13	This module is pure: no I/O, no CSV, no filesystem.
    14	"""
    15	
    16	import functools
    17	from fractions import Fraction
    18	from typing import Optional, Tuple

    20	POINTS_FOR_WIN = 2
    21	POINTS_FOR_DRAW = 1
    22	POINTS_FOR_LOSS = 0
    23	
    24	
    25	def points_for_result(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> int:
    26	    """Points a club earns from a single match with this scoreline."""
    27	    if goals_for > goals_against:
    28	        return POINTS_FOR_WIN
    29	    if goals_for == goals_against:
    30	        return POINTS_FOR_DRAW
    31	    return POINTS_FOR_LOSS
    32	
    33	
    34	# Ranking bands for the two degenerate cases of a ratio. A club that has
    35	# conceded nothing has an unbounded average and so outranks every finite one; a
    36	# club with no goals either way (including one that has played no games) has no
    37	# average at all -- 0/0 -- and cannot outrank a club that achieved a real ratio.
    38	# Both are deliberate choices, not accidents of arithmetic; see README.md.
    39	_UNDEFINED = 0
    40	_FINITE = 1
    41	_INFINITE = 2
    42	
    43	
    44	@functools.total_ordering
    45	class GoalAverage:
    46	    """Goals scored divided by goals conceded, as a totally ordered value.
    47	
    48	    Held as a `Fraction`, never a float: averages like 14/11 and 25/19 are
    49	    compared directly against one another, and float rounding would silently
    50	    equate values that differ, mis-ordering the table.
    51	
    52	    Greater is better, so these objects sort the way the league table reads.
    53	    """
    54	
    55	    __slots__ = ("_band", "_value")
    56	
    57	    def __init__(self, goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> None:
    58	        if goals_against > 0:
    59	            self._band = _FINITE
    60	            self._value: Optional[Fraction] = Fraction(goals_for, goals_against)
    61	        elif goals_for > 0:
    62	            self._band = _INFINITE
    63	            self._value = None
    64	        else:
    65	            self._band = _UNDEFINED
    66	            self._value = None
    67	
    68	    @property
    69	    def value(self) -> Optional[Fraction]:
    70	        """The exact ratio, or None where it is infinite or undefined."""
    71	        return self._value
    72	
    73	    @property
    74	    def is_infinite(self) -> bool:
    75	        """True when the club has scored but conceded nothing."""
    76	        return self._band == _INFINITE
    77	
    78	    @property
    79	    def is_undefined(self) -> bool:
    80	        """True when the club has neither scored nor conceded (0/0)."""
    81	        return self._band == _UNDEFINED
    82	
    83	    def sort_key(self) -> Tuple[int, Fraction]:

    85	
    86	        Both numbers negate cleanly, so a caller wanting descending order can
    87	        simply negate them rather than wrap this in a reversing adapter.
    88	        """
    89	        ratio = Fraction(0) if self._value is None else self._value

     2	
     3	Pure domain: no I/O. The rules it applies live in `rules.py`.
     4	
     5	The tool knows nothing about weeks, matchdays or the 1974/75 season. It
     6	tabulates whatever results it is given; deciding *which* results constitute
     7	"the 10th week" is the caller's business, and is discussed in README.md.
     8	"""
     9	
    10	from typing import Dict, Iterable, List, Tuple
    11	
    12	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    13	from league_table.rules import goal_average, points_for_result

    16	class _Tally:
    17	    """A club's running totals while the results are being read."""
    18	
    19	    __slots__ = ("won", "drawn", "lost", "goals_for", "goals_against", "points")
    20	
    21	    def __init__(self) -> None:
    22	        self.won = 0
    23	        self.drawn = 0
    24	        self.lost = 0
    25	        self.goals_for = 0
    26	        self.goals_against = 0
    27	        self.points = 0
    28	
    29	    def record(self, scored: int, conceded: int) -> None:
    30	        self.goals_for += scored
    31	        self.goals_against += conceded
    32	        self.points += points_for_result(scored, conceded)
    33	
    34	        if scored > conceded:
    35	            self.won += 1
    36	        elif scored == conceded:
    37	            self.drawn += 1
    38	        else:
    39	            self.lost += 1
    40	
    41	    @property
    42	    def played(self) -> int:
    43	        return self.won + self.drawn + self.lost
    44	
    45	
    46	def _ranking_key(entry: Tuple[str, _Tally]):
    47	    """The 1974/75 order: points, then goal average, then club name.
    48	
    49	    Points and the goal average both sort descending -- higher is better -- and
    50	    both are plain numbers, so they are simply negated. The club name breaks the
    51	    remaining ties ascending.
    52	
    53	    The name is not a historical rule. The Football League had no tie-break
    54	    beyond goal average; clubs level on both genuinely shared the position. It is
    55	    here so the output is deterministic, and it is documented as such.
    56	    """
    57	    team, tally = entry
    58	    band, ratio = goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against).sort_key()
    59	    return (-tally.points, -band, -ratio, team)
    60	
    61	
    62	def build_table(matches: Iterable[Match]) -> List[Standing]:
    63	    """The standing table for these results, best club first."""
    64	    tallies: Dict[str, _Tally] = {}
    65	
    66	    for match in matches:
    67	        home = tallies.setdefault(match.home_team, _Tally())
    68	        away = tallies.setdefault(match.away_team, _Tally())
    69	
    70	        home.record(match.home_goals, match.away_goals)
    71	        away.record(match.away_goals, match.home_goals)
    72	
    73	    ranked = sorted(tallies.items(), key=_ranking_key)
    74	
    75	    return [
    76	        Standing(
    77	            position=position,
    78	            team=team,
    79	            played=tally.played,
    80	            won=tally.won,
    81	            drawn=tally.drawn,
    82	            lost=tally.lost,
    83	            goals_for=tally.goals_for,
    84	            goals_against=tally.goals_against,
    85	            goal_average=goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against),
    86	            points=tally.points,
    87	        )
    88	        for position, (team, tally) in enumerate(ranked, start=1)
    89	    ]

trong First Division side.

Blackburn Rovers, Plymouth Argyle and Charlton Athletic occupied the three promotion places in the Third Division.

Going down were AFC Bournemouth, Tranmere Rovers, Watford and Huddersfield Town. This meant that Huddersfield would be playing Fourth Division football for the first time in their history, the first former English champions so to do. Watford, meanwhile, were enduring their last unsuccessful season for many years to come.

Mansfield Town, Shrewsbury Town, Rotherham United and Chester occupied the four promotion places in the Fourth Division. Chester had finally managed promotion after 44 years of league membership, narrowly edging out Lincoln City on goal average.

Scunthorpe United, who had narrowly missed out on top-division football during the 1960s, found themselves bottom of the league but retained their league status after being re-elected along with the three clubs placed above them.

def test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding():
    """Goal average is a Fraction, not a float.

    An in-principle test, and deliberately absurd: it takes goal counts no club
    could ever record to force a float64 collision. At real scorelines floats
    would cope fine -- 14/11 and 25/19 are separated by about fifteen digits.

    The point is not that floats would break on 1974/75 data. It is that with
    Fractions the ordering is exact by construction rather than exact by luck,
    and its correctness does not depend on how big the inputs happen to be.

    Both values below are 0.3333333333333333 in IEEE-754 double precision, so a
    float implementation calls them equal and mis-orders the clubs. Exactly,
    1/3 is the greater.
    """
    exact_third = goal_average(1, 3)
    just_under_a_third = goal_average(33_333_333_333_333_333, 100_000_000_000_000_000)

    assert float(exact_third.value) == float(just_under_a_third.value)  # floats agree
    assert exact_third > just_under_a_third  # fractions do not

content.content:
=====
 **The uncovered line was dead code.** `_Descending.__eq__` is never called — `sorted` only ever needs `__lt__`. Rather than pragma it, I'm deleting it, which is what I said I'd do if a pragma started looking tempting.
=====
 That failure is a real bug I introduced — and it's the best possible argument for the TDD order.

Deleting `_Descending.__eq__` looked safe ("`sorted` only needs `__lt__`"). It isn't. Python compares tuples element-by-element using `==` *first*, and only falls through to the next element when two are equal. With no `__eq__`, two `_Descending` wrappers holding identical goal averages compared as *unequal* (identity fallback), so the sort never reached the club-name element — the alphabetical tie-break was silently unreachable. Coverage had flagged the line as untested; my inference that "untested means dead" was wrong, and the test proved it in about ten seconds.

Restoring it, without the `i
=====
 Done. The tool is built, tested, and the 1974/75 answer is computed and independently verified.

## What's there

79 tests, 100% branch coverage enforced by the test run (`pytest` fails below it), one excluded line — the `if __name__ == "__main__":` entry point. Built strictly test-first: every module went red before it went green.

```
src/league_table/  rules.py (points, goal average, ordering — pure, no I/O)
                   table.py, models.py, csvio.py, cli.py
data/              two input CSVs + two output CSVs
README.md  CLAUDE.md  AI_REFLECTION.md  .claude/  ai/
```

**The answer** — English First Division, after 10 matchdays (28 Sep 1974): Ipswich Town top on 16 points with a 3.000
=====
 QA's nine bugs are fixed and the suite is green: 92 tests, 100% branch coverage, and both real 1974/75 tables are byte-identical to before the refactor.

## What QA found, and what I did

The two that mattered most were **silent wrong answers**. A duplicated `home_goals` column in the header resolved last-one-wins and produced a plausible table with the wrong numbers at exit 0 — now rejected. Deleting `_Descending.__eq__` looked safe ("`sorted` only needs `__lt__`"). It isn't. content.text:
All sources opened and every claim checked. Here is the report.

## FALSE / MISLEADING claims (most serious first)

**1. README.md:316 — wrong position for Liverpool in our own table.**
> "it shows Liverpool 7th where we have them 4th"

FALSE. Our matchday-10 table has **Liverpool 5th**, not 4th (`data/standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv`: 4 = Middlesbrough, 5 = Liverpool). worldfootball does show Liverpool 7th, so only the second half is wrong. It also contradicts README.md:108, which correctly says "Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th."

This is **leftover wreckage from the retracted worldfootball claim**. The session transcript shows the original false sentence read "Liverpool appears 7th there and 4th here". When the goal-difference explanation was deleted, the "4th" travelled with the surviving clause. Correction: "…where we have them **5th**."

**2. src/league_table/rules.py:49–51 — the float claim the README explicitly disowns.**
> "Held as a `Fraction`, never a float: averages like 14/11 and 25/19 are compared directly against one another, and float rounding would silently equate values that differ, mis-ordering the table."

FALSE, and it is the *same overclaim the README already corrected elsewhere*. In IEEE-754 doubles, 14/11 = 1.2727272727272727 and 25/19 = 1.3157894736842106 — not remotely equal. README.md:154–158 says so outright ("At football scorelines they would not… separated by about fifteen digits"), and the test's own docstring in `tests/test_rules_goal_average.py` repeats the honest version. Only the source docstring still carries the false one. Correction: restate it as the README does — `Fraction` makes the ordering exact by construction, independent of the magnitude of the inputs; it is not that floats would break at football scorelines.

**3. README.md:166 — 31 August 1974 was not a midweek fixture.**
> "The Football League squeezed extra midweek rounds into the opening weeks (19–21 Aug, 27–28 Aug, 31 Aug)"

MISLEADING. 31 August 1974 was a **Saturday**, and the project's own data has all 11 round-5 fixtures on that date. The genuinely midweek rounds are round 2 (Mon 19 – Wed 21 Aug) and round 4 (Tue 27 – Wed 28 Aug). The substantive point survives — five rounds were played in the first 15 days — but 31 Aug should be dropped from the list of midweek dates or relabelled.

**4. README.md:85–86 — the Wikipedia quote is misquoted.**
> *"This was the first league season with three points for a win."*

The actual sentence on the 1981–82 Football League page is "This was the first league season with three points for **win**" — no "a". The substance is correct and the page's next sentence says "Three points for a win was introduced for the first time in England," which would serve as a clean verbatim quote. But a sentence presented in italics as a quotation should match the source character for character, especially in a document whose whole thesis is that sourced claims must be checked.

**5. README.md:218–220 — "orders the clubs by it" is an inference, not something the page proves.**
> "[11v11] prints its own **goal average** column and orders the clubs by it."

Not false, but it is the same *kind* of claim as the retracted worldfootball one, and it is not established by the page. 11v11's table prints **both** a GAvg and a GD column (the README never mentions the GD column). Goal average descends strictly within every points block, so it fully explains the order — but goal-difference-then-alphabetical also reproduces the observed order exactly, because nowhere on that page do the two rules disagree. By the project's own evidentiary standard, this should say the order is *consistent with* goal average, not that 11v11 sorts by it.

**6. README.md:68 — the `| head` example does not actually produce exit 141.**
> "141 | stdout was closed early (`| head`)"

The behaviour is real — I confirmed a genuinely closed pipe yields exit 141 with no traceback — but `league-table data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv | head -1` exits **0**, because the ~1 KB table fits entirely in the 64 KB pipe buffer and the write never fails. I had to generate a 1.4 MB table to trigger 141. The claim is defensible as written ("stdout was closed early"), but the parenthetical example doesn't reproduce it.

## Verified TRUE

**History (Wikipedia, opened directly).** Two points for a win in 1974/75 and three points from 1981/82: the 1981–82 page states three points for a win was introduced for the first time in England that season. Goal average as the tie-break, and its abolition: the 1974–75 page carries, **verbatim**, "Beginning with the season 1894–95, clubs finishing level on points were separated according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)" and "The goal average system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season." The README's ellipsis elides only "or more properly put, goal ratio" and a sentence about goals-scored, which does not distort the meaning. The same rules asserted in CLAUDE.md and `rules.py`'s module docstring are correct.

**11v11, 19 October 1974 — the headline claim.** I opened the page. Its table has a goal-average column, and `data/standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv` reproduces it **exactly**: all 22 rows, same order, same averages to three decimals, Liverpool top on 19 points from 13 games ahead of Manchester City on 19 from 14, Leicester on 12 games. Every figure quoted in README.md:224–231 matches.

**The hedge at README.md:240–244 is accurate.** Burnley (23:23) / Newcastle (19:19) and Birmingham (20:20) / Wolverhampton (15:15) are exact 1.000 ties. Alphabetical order and goals-scored order both put Burnley above Newcastle and Birmingham above Wolves, so the data genuinely cannot distinguish the rules. The refusal to claim more is correct.

**11v11, 28 September 1974.** Opened. All 22 rows match `data/standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` exactly — in fact more than the README claims. The sixteen P=10 clubs agree with the matchday-10 table, and the six P=9 clubs (Newcastle, Middlesbrough, Leicester, Leeds, Arsenal, Tottenham) each differ by exactly one result with no leftover goals or points, reconstructing precisely the three postponed round-9 fixtures.

**worldfootball matchday 10.** Opened. All 22 clubs agree with ours on played/won/drawn/lost/goals-for/goals-against/points. Its 13-point block runs Middlesbrough, Everton, Sheffield United, Liverpool — matching neither goal difference nor goal average, exactly as the README says. The refusal to claim a sort rule is correct.

**Fixture calendar (checked against the data files).** Season opened Saturday 17 Aug 1974; round 10 completed entirely on Saturday 28 Sep 1974; round 14 entirely on Saturday 19 Oct 1974, which falls in the 10th Saturday-to-Friday week (19–25 Oct); clubs on 12–14 games at that point (1 on 12, 10 on 13, 11 on 14). Six postponed fixtures, three from round 9 and three from round 13. The three round-9 ones carry exactly the dates claimed: Leeds v Tottenham 4 Dec 1974, Middlesbrough v Leicester 10 Dec 1974, Newcastle v Arsenal 23 Apr 1975. File sizes match: 110 / 107 / 148 matches.

**Provenance.** The 22 club names in the data are an exact set-match with Wikipedia's final 1974–75 First Division table (whose 462 matches confirm 22 clubs).

**Code and tests.** `pytest` gives **99 passed, 100% branch coverage** (261 statements, 54 branches, zero missed), and `pyproject.toml`'s only `exclude_lines` entry is `if __name__ == "__main__":`. I exercised the CLI directly: exit 0 on success; exit 2 with the offending line number for a missing file, `"1_0"` scores, a duplicated `home_goals` header, and a wrong-width row; BOM files parse; the duplicate-fixture warning fires on stderr while still exiting 0; the in-place rewrite (`-o` same as input) is safe; exit 141 on a genuinely closed pipe; and regenerating the matchday-10 table byte-matches the committed CSV. The Middlesbrough/Liverpool goal-average-vs-goal-difference reversal is real (2.143 vs 2.125 against +8 vs +9), and Tottenham/Arsenal/QPR all sit on 6 points with an identical −5 goal difference and averages 0.706 / 0.667 / 0.615. All four tests named in the README exist. `Standing` has no goal-difference field.

**AI_REFLECTION's deleted-method story checks out.** The transcript confirms it: the AI deleted `_Descending.__eq__` from `table.py` after coverage flagged it, tuple comparison then fell back to identity, and the club-name tie-break for clubs level on points *and* goal average became unreachable — precisely as described. One caveat: `_Descending` no longer exists (`_ranking_key` at table.py:58–59 now negates a plain `sort_key()` tuple), so a reviewer checking today's code will not find the method. `GoalAverage.__eq__` at rules.py:92 still exists but is **not** load-bearing for the sort, so anyone verifying the commit message 5ad3f04's bare "`__eq__`" against `rules.py` would wrongly conclude the story is false. Naming the class in the commit message would prevent that.

## Unverifiable

README.md:178–180's argument that shifting the week boundary "moves the answer from round 13 to round 14" is rhetorical rather than checkable — it depends on which week convention you pick, and under both Saturday-to-Friday and Monday-to-Sunday counting from kickoff the 10th week lands on round 14. The broader point (the calendar reading is convention-dependent) stands; the specific round-13/round-14 illustration is not one I could reproduce.

I changed nothing in the project directory. Scratch files are in `/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-jramirezolmos-Documents-personal-span-league-table/ed232752-4a2f-47aa-9632-c8175fcab651/scratchpad`.
